metadata.title: The identity‐attitude nexus in the representation of energy transition in a coal region (Sulcis, Italy): An exploration through the Structural Topic Model

abstract: This article explores the contribution of the Structural Topic Model (STM) to study the intertwining of social representations, attitudes, and identities. We examine newspapers' discourse on energy transition in a coal-dependent region (Sulcis, Italy), whose identity and economy are built around mining and carbon-intensive industry. Drawing upon Social Representations Theory, we combined STM and qualitative content analysis to examine how newspapers represented the energy issue in Sulcis, and how these representations (including denotative and connotative facets) differed according to the social identities' salience. Results show that coal is legitimated by discourses making salient local identities and providing continuity in the place-identity link, with no alternative to extractivism. When the Sardinian superordinate identity becomes salient, new themes emerge (e.g., renewables) but energy transition retains ambivalent or negative connotations. Overall representations seem to replicate and reflect processes of domination and injustice hindering a just transition, potentially explaining negative attitudes and collective resistance to decarbonization.

As a result, increasingly complex models are currently used to explain and predict attitudes and behavioural intentions, taking into account the features of sustainable energy technologies (e.g., perceived costs and risks), the contextual factors (e.g., perceived trust and fairness) and the individual variables (e.g., perceived control, personal norms) (Huijts et al., 2012).

Furthermore, relevant authors in the field stress that sociopsychological knowledge should integrate and build upon system analyses to understand the structural factors affecting individuals and groups acting in the vets of consumers, users, producers, and political agents and levers of change (Krupnik et al., 2022;Nielsen et al., 2021;Stern, 2020;Wullenkord & Hamann, 2021). These contributions examine the sustainable energy transition within systemic frameworks (e.g., multi-level perspectives, socio-technical transition, the energy culture model) and aim at understanding the meaning-making processes at the interface between individual and social as a function of historical events and political agendas (Sarrica et al., 2016).

Following this societal perspective, and more specifically adopting the Social Representation Theory (SRT) (Sammut et al., 2015) as an overarching framework, in this contribution we aim to explore the ways in which energy issues, their situated connotations, and the connected identities are socially constructed in a coal region in transition.

Moreover, the present paper builds upon previous studies on Social Representations (SRs) of energy transitions to add a methodological refinement based on natural language processing (NLP), as a way to analyse representational processes and the related identity positioning in large amounts of textual data.

In the following sections, we will first address the topic of attitudes and identity in the literature on energy transition. We will then briefly introduce the theory of social representations and its heuristic potential in the field. Finally, drawing upon a study conducted on the way newspapers are handling the topic of sustainable energy transition in one coal and carbon-intensive region in Italy, we will demonstrate NLP's contribution to the study of social representations, attitudes, and identity issues.

sections.header_raw: ENERGY TRANSITION, ATTITUDE, AND IDENTITY ISSUES
sections.header_norm: energy transition attitude and identity issues
paragraphs: The human dimension of energy 'refers to the rich mixture of cultural practices, social interactions, and human feelings that influence the behavior of individuals, social groups, and public and private institutions' (Stern & Aronson, 1984, p. 2). Adopting this broad perspective, psychological research has so far addressed processes that affect energy transitions across multiple levels (cf. Biddau et al., 2022).

The first strand of research focuses on individual determinants of sustainable behaviours, on psychosocial processes leading to acceptance of and support for interventions promoting sustainability, and on understanding public and political support for energy system transformations (i.e., policies, technologies, changes to a place, etc.) (Huijts et al., 2012). Acceptance and identity issues have been examined further, as they intersect with the symbolic, affective, and identity aspects of places affected by infrastructural development (Devine-Wright, 2009). The psychological impact in relation to the transformation of significant places has been portrayed as a form of disruption to place attachment and identity (Bonaiuto et al., 1996;Brown & Perkins, 1992). Empirical evidence demonstrated that the siting of renewable energy facilities may be perceived by community residents as an 'inappropriate development' (Devine-Wright & Howes, 2010) that threatens the distinctiveness of local places and evokes feelings of disruption. Finally, energy transitions are inherently political, highly contested processes where different actors hold different views about desirable directions for transformation and appropriate ways to govern such processes (Adil & Ko, 2016;Geels, 2005;Kuzemko et al., 2016). Therefore, the research analysed the multiple intergroup relations and associated identities playing a key role in the emergence and consolidation of specific attitudes and coping responses about energy policies and technologies (Colvin, 2020). From there, most literature on the social acceptance of renewables has focused on the relationship between developers, decision-makers, and local community members as the main intergroup conflict (Batel & Devine-Wright, 2018), revealing how infrastructural projects can be interpreted as an 'aggression' from actors external to the community, or as 'contradictory' to the distinctiveness of the place (and related identity) (Barry et al., 2008;Van der Horst, 2007).

Although the acceptance of renewables and the associated infrastructures and energy policies remain a key component of energy transition scholarship, nowadays there is an increasing awareness that to accelerate decarbonization it is also necessary to address the flip side of the coin, namely the destabilization, decline, and phase-out of fossil fuels with particular attention to coal-and carbon-intensive areas. Such systemic thinking is necessary for creating space and gaining momentum for the diffusion of sustainable energy technologies and policies (Köhler et al., 2019;Loorbach et al., 2017;Rinscheid et al., 2021). On this matter, several studies found that many fossil fuel communities have a deeply ingrained connection with the extractive industry, as well as cultural bonds that extend well beyond economic reliance, influencing the local understanding and attitudes towards the energy transition and particularly towards phase-out processes (e.g., Cha, 2020). In this perspective, the work by Della Bosca and Gillespie (2018) highlighted how coal plays a major role in the development of many Australian communities. Citizens living in these coal areas develop an emotional connection between coal and local identity. Residents view coal mining as the main source of regional identity, as the basis for people-place connection, and as providing a sense of continuity for personal and familial identity. As stressed by Mayer (2018), mining communities show a profound nostalgia for the golden age of mining. This phenomenon is often rooted in deliberate efforts by the extractive industries to construct a community economic identity (cf. Bell & Yorke, 2010) through cultural manipulation, that is, by forging a situation in which an industry becomes the core of a community's sense of self. The effects are brought to light for example by Mason et al. (2014), who found that attitudes towards mining in Australia can vary according to shifts in social identities. When a higher level of categorization (national identity) was salient, the foreigners were the participants' salient outgroup, and the mining companies were locally considered as preserving the Australian's economy and employment.

On the other hand, when a lower-level categorization was more salient (e.g., stewards of Australia's natural environment) the participants expressed antagonistic attitudes towards the extractive industry. Similar results have been found in other coal-based contexts. Research conducted in Poland found that the national identification was related to support for anti-conservation policies (i.e., subsidies for the coal industry, deforestation plans) only when associated with a collective narcissistic component-that is, a defensive form of ingroup identification based on the belief that others (in this case, the European Union) do not appreciate, recognize, or value the ingroup. As a result, citizens develop an instrumental view of nature, which can be exploited to bolster the group's image as strong and independent from policies forced upon the ingroup (Cislak et al., 2018).

In sum, we may say that in coal communities and regions interested by phase-out policies, intergroup dynamics, power issues, and cultural processes give rise to a multiplicity of meanings about what the transition actually is, and how it relates with relevant social identities.

Navigating a destabilization-reconfiguration pathway thus requires careful political and cultural action able to consider intertwined sociopolitical and socio-psychological challenges and consequences (Biddau et al., 2022;Rogge & Johnstone, 2017). Social representations theory proved useful in understanding such situated interactions between the socio-political and socio-psychological dimensions.

Rizzoli et al. - 2024 - The identity‐attitude nexus in the representation of energy transition in a coal region (Sulcis, Ita
paragraphs: Social Representation Theory, or SRT, is a mild form of socioconstructivism focusing primarily in the way in which individuals and communities make sense of important but unfamiliar events or transformations. According to the theory, people engage in cognitive coping processes with the aim of interpreting and mastering changes. The result of these meaning-making processes are social representations (SRs), that is, 'system of values, ideas and practices with a twofold function: first, to establish an order which will enable individuals to orient themselves in their material and social world and to master it;

and second, to enable communication' (Moscovici, 1973, p. xiii). Pivotal to SRT is the assumption that the representations associated with an object are not elaborated by individuals in a vacuum, but are always the result of the interaction between an 'ego' and an 'alter' . A number of corollaries derive from this assumption. First, attitudes and behaviours relate to objects that are the result of social construction processes, and which should thus be considered as part and parcel of SRs (Doise, 1989). Accordingly, attitude can be examined as the evaluative component of the representation (Moliner & Tafani, 1997), and social behaviours as constitutive of representations, and neither is their consequence nor an epiphenomenon (Wagner, 2017). Second, having inherited the lesson of symbolic interactionism, SRT assumes that ego-alter interaction simultaneously refers to the specific other and to the generalized other (Castro, 2015). The dynamics of representation can thus be examined in its microgenesis, sociogenesis, and ontogenesis, that is, looking at the situated interaction, at the circulation and negotiations at societal and intergroup levels, and at the cultural absorption during individual socialization (Flick et al., 2015;Psaltis, 2015). Finally, denotative and connotative meanings of a social object are 'the outcome of the processes of recursive social semiosis within and between social groups in the public sphere' (Veltri, 2015, p. 243). Therefore, in order to understand SRs it appears necessary to take into account the social identities of the actors engaged in the public arena, as well as their power relationship (Breakwell, 2011;Castro & Batel, 2008;Howarth, 2006).

First, groups can be objects of representations that define their characteristics either directly, or indirectly, through defining what they are and what they are not (Breakwell, 1993). By constructing shared views of 'who we are' and 'who they are' , groups maintain or contest inequalities, build positive self-image, (dis)empower communities (Elcheroth et al., 2011;Howarth, 2006;Oyserman & Markus, 1998). These representations are clearly intertwined with the respective access to the public arena, and different actors exert different levels of power in the process of constructing others: subordinated groups are often represented in terms of aggregates compiled by depersonalized and interchangeable others (Lorenzi-Cioldi, 1998), and in extreme cases this representation can lead to the construction of 'stigma theories' justifying processes of social exclusion (Howarth, 2001). Moreover, it is worth noting that self-other identities and the representations of the objects at stake stand in a mutually constitutive relationship. Multiple representations coexist in the public arena, either converging in defining the object at stake or presenting conflicts about its nature (Bauer & Gaskell, 2008). At the same time, sharing a SR implies a positioning of self and others and an assessment of the validity of competing representations. Thus, identities act as anchors for interpretative and communicative activity (Howarth, 2006), and are continuously produced through positioning in communication. That is, individuals are relationally engaged in 'doing identities' , which can be seen as 'positions in relation to social representations' (Andreouli, 2010, p. 14). As a result, whereas stability in self-other relationships helps to reproduce established representations, a change in one element of the semiotic triangle (ego-alter-object) may contribute to a radical shift in a given representation, and to a deep reconsideration of the intergroup positionings and attitudes towards the objects at stake (Psaltis, 2012).

paragraphs: Drawing on these premises, SRT goes beyond the 'impact' or 'acceptance' frameworks in the study of how society and technology are related (e.g., Brondi & Neresini, 2018). For this reason, SRT has been largely used in exploring the social construction of decarbonization and energy transition in the public arena, as well as their power relations, identity, and attitudinal processes (Batel & Devine-Wright, 2015;Devine-Wright & Devine-Wright, 2009;Sarrica et al., 2015;Upham et al., 2015). Together with acceptance studies, SRT has been adopted to explore specific representations of renewable energy technologies, focusing on individual technologies (e.g., solar, wind)
or on their joint presence as new and multifaceted objects, against which the communities under investigation are confronted (Cotton & Devine-Wright, 2009;Devine-Wright, 2005;Sarrica et al., 2015).

Looking at the policies on energy transition, the studies also examined underlying representations expressed by policymakers in discourses and regulations. Research showed, for example, an emphasis on the representation of energy as an economic resource, and the permanence of deficit views of users (Brondi et al., 2016;Devine-Wright & Devine-Wright, 2005;Sarrica et al., 2014). The view on users, described as lacking in agency and knowledge, appears far removed from the ideal model of energy citizenship that should underpin a mature transition (Devine-Wright, 2007;Lennon et al., 2019;Sarrica et al., 2018). Finally, the SRT has been regarded as a theory of change, whose goal is understanding the more systemic aspects of the transition. In this sense, both identity and power dimensions have been brought to the fore, demonstrating how 'we must acknowledge that not all representations and associated identities are equally powerful, and the NIMBY [not in my backyard] literature makes a good case for illustrating this, while revealing how experts and decision-makers are often able to exclude local communities' perspectives from the decision-making processes affecting them' (Batel & Devine-Wright, 2015, p. 319). A critical reading of SRT applied to environmental and energy issues, in short, stressed the need not only to explore different representations of energy, users, and the energy system (Stern & Aronson, 1984), but also to reflect on who puts forward alternative SRs and why (Batel et al., 2016;Caillaud, 2016;Sarrica et al., 2016).

Research shows that such visions are often structured along a few fundamental thematical lines-for example, anthropocentric versus ecocentric; deficit versus capabilities; horizontal versus vertical relationships (see e.g., Brondi et al., 2014)-and contribute to the definition of spaces of voice and action for the different groups involved in the transition.

In this sense, the most recent proposals merge SRT with critical and post-colonial studies, in an effort to shed light on processes of domination and injustice embedded in ongoing transition processes (Batel & Devine-Wright, 2016;Lennon, 2017). On the one hand, research conducted from a SRT perspective converges with other emerging reflections in the social sciences and emphasizes how experimentation, financialization, and dispossession processes, which are at the core of extractivist models, are still strategically employed in elite discourses to marginalize the voices of local citizens and to maintain the control of the ongoing decarbonization (Sovacool et al., 2019). On the other hand, however, the multi-scalar nature of transitions suggests that the representational dynamic should be critically examined by taking into account the articulation between multiple levels, from the international policies to the situated discourses where the dominant perspectives can be adopted, but also adapted or resisted Sarrica et al. (2018). For these reasons, studying the representations mobilized in the context of decarbonization processes within a coal region appears particularly relevant, both to examine the specific contents emerging within ongoing processes of change, and to explore how self-other identities and power relationships are manifested in constructing the object 'energy transition' , and to what extent these processes are replicating or subverting culturally rooted mechanisms of domination.

sections.paper_id: Rizzoli et al. - 2024 - The identity‐attitude nexus in the representation of energy transition in a coal region (Sulcis, Ita
paragraphs: The region of Sulcis is an area in the south-west of Sardinia, one of the 20 administrative regions in Italy. Sulcis is a prototype of a coal and carbon-intensive region, that is, a subnational territory with high economic dependency on fossil fuel extraction such as coal mining and carbon-intensive industries such as steel (European Commission, 2018). In fact, the region has a long mining history (coal, lead, zinc) that constitutes a distinctive feature of the regional economy, iden- Energy production and distribution were designed and developed according to the availability of coal and the presence of logistic infrastructure (e.g., the port) for transporting coal (as coal extracted in Sulcis is highly impure and needs to be mixed for energy generation) and metals (importing raw materials, exporting processed goods).

In the last decades, because of the increase in energy prices and the global crisis affecting the metal-processing sector too, the region suffered a severe industrial decline leading to high rates of unemployment, job losses, emigration, and a decrease in the resident population, especially among younger generations. Since 2012, dedicated investment plans have been put in place to solve the socio-economic crisis (e.g., Piano Sulcis,foot_2 2012, 1.243 mln Euro), but so far they have not given the expected positive outcomes. Beyond its industrial decline, the region is now the object of coal phase-out policies, which cause uncertainties about energy security and socio-economic stability, as the region is dependent on coal for generating around 70% of its energy and lacks the essential infrastructure for natural gas. For these reasons, the Sulcis region is considered eligible for the Just Transition Fund to mitigate the socio-economic impact of decarbonization, and a number of renewable energy investments and projects are currently being considered both by the government and by large companies and operators of the energy sector.

In this regard, the identity of Sulcis is historically based on industrial practices (extraction, transformation) and places (mines, coal-fired powerplants, industrial clusters close to coal-based facilities). Therefore, the transformation of places, practices, and sources of income poses a threat to regional livelihoods, and requires disruptive changes in identity structures and community capacity to imagine itself into the F I G U R E 1 Sulcis coal and carbon-intensive territory. From right to left: Sulcis province, Sulcis coal basin, Sulcis carbon-intensive industrial cluster.

paragraphs: At a societal level, the meaning-making process can be studied starting from media communication (Batel & Castro, 2015). The study of newspapers' discourse provides us with insights into the politics and legitimacy of transitions beyond formal policymaking (Markard et al., 2021), as they represent both the context in which conflicts and controversies could be reconstructed and the context through which conflicts can be enacted (Carvalho, 2008;Castro et al., 2018). Therefore, the newspapers articles are an important source of natural data from a perspective that considers multiple levels of analysis (Sarrica et al., 2018). Moreover, the press is commonly viewed as an authoritative source, and thus newspapers become actors of the signification process.

sections.paper_id: Rizzoli et al. - 2024 - The identity‐attitude nexus in the representation of energy transition in a coal region (Sulcis, Ita
sections.header_norm: the corpus and pre processing phase
paragraphs: We identified Italian newspapers that deal with the energy transition in Sulcis at different levels, corresponding to potential different perspectives on the issue. We selected Ansa (the main multimedia Italian news agency) at national level, La Nuova Sardegna (the most widespread newspaper in northern Sardinia) and L'Unione Sarda (the oldest and most widespread newspaper in Sardinia, in general) at administrative/regional level (i.e., Sardinia region), and La Provincia del Sulcis-Iglesiente, as the local newspaper that only covers the area of interest. Moreover, La Nuova Sardegna and L'Unione Sarda historically reflect the regional political debate by explicitly positioning themselves on opposite poles, that is, respectively left-leaning and right wing. Through their online databasesfoot_4 we collected all the articles (title, subtitle, and text body) containing the keywords 'energ*' and 'Sulcis' published in a 10-year timespan (from 1 January 2011 to 28 February 2021). The selected period coincided with the diffusion of multiple renewable energy projects as well as phase-out decisions on mining and coal-fired power generation. The corpus consisted of 965 articles,foot_5 manually checked for relevance to the energy issue during the collection (e.g., we excluded energy meant as physical energy in sports).

We pre-processed the corpus through the TalTac2 software (Bolasco et al., 2000) to verify whether its size was sufficient to apply analyses based on bags-of-words approaches (i.e., on words count) and to identify multiwords (i.e., meaningful sequences of words such as economic development). The corpus is composed of 308,288 occurrences (i.e., token) and 19,838 distinct forms (i.e., type). Since the percentage of forms that appear only once (hapaxes) is less than 50% (42.3%) and the type/token ratio less than 20% (6.4%), the corpus can be considered adequate to be analysed with these methods (cf. Lebart et al., 1998).
We then applied a procedure for multiwords identification following Pavone (2018) to consider each sequence of words with frequency ≥5 as a single textual unit.

sections.paper_id: Rizzoli et al. - 2024 - The identity‐attitude nexus in the representation of energy transition in a coal region (Sulcis, Ita
paragraphs: The analyses combine the tools of NLP and the interpretative key of social psychology, particularly SRT, in two steps. First, the Structural Topic Model (STM; Roberts et al., 2019)-implemented in the stm R package-was applied in order to identify which topics are debated in the articles. This passage brings to light the different perspectives on the energy issue (i.e., denotative component of the representation). Topic detection and extraction has already been used in the SRs arena to identify the contents of a representation and their articulation (e.g., Lynam, 2016;Manchaiah et al., 2018;Polli & Camargo, 2015;Sarrica et al., 2020). It allows for a systematic synthesis of a large number of texts that would have otherwise been unmanageable.

Instead of other methods of topic detection, including those more common in the field (Reinert, 1983), we decided to use STM because it allows a more context-based interpretation of the corpus by including covariates in estimating the topic model. In our study, the covariate is represented by the geographical scale of newspapers operationalized as levels (i.e., who shares a certain perspective on the issue) in interaction with the time variable 'year' (to account for significant moments in the energy transition included in the time-span selected). This implies that we assume the topical prevalence (i.e., the probability that a

given document belongs to a specific topic) can be affected by certain factors-levels in interaction with time in our case-and includes the possibility to estimate such effect. In terms of SRT, by assuming the coexistence of different representations in the public arena (Bauer & Gaskell, 2008), our approach allows both to identify these representations while uncovering their structure and relationships, and to examine how these representations interact with various factors and with each other in contributing to their definition. In other terms, it means taking into account the multifaceted nature of the object of representation. Precisely because of this reason, this topic model proved to be among the most promising in the social sciences' application (e.g., Roberts et al., 2013Roberts et al., , 2016)), and it is starting to gain traction within the field of SRT as well (cf. De Rosa et al., 2023).

Adopting the same assumptions of the probabilistic topic models, such as the most famous Latent Dirichlet Allocation (Blei et al., 2003), 'a topic is defined as a mixture over words where each word has a probability of belonging to a topic' (Robert et al., 2019, p. 2) and a topic has a probability of belonging to a document (i.e., article, in our case). Therefore, each article can be composed of multiple topics, each of which has a different probability of being part of that article. In the STM, this probability can vary across texts' metadata (i.e., levels in our case).

First, to select a topic model for a fixed number of topics we ran a number of models from five to 30 topics through a function available in the stm R package, estimating several parameters. These parameters include the held-out likelihood, which measures the accuracy of each model in predicting words within the documents (Wallach et al., 2009). We also examined residuals to determine if there was excessive variation in the distribution of words predicted by the STM within the data (Taddy, 2012). Furthermore, we considered semantic coherence, which indicates the likelihood of words appearing together within a specific topic (Mimno et al., 2011). Additionally, we assessed exclusivity, which addresses the issue of achieving high semantic coherence too easily by having a small number of topics dominated by extremely common words (Roberts et al., 2014;Bischof & Airoldi, 2012) (Figure 2).

There is never a correct number of topics, the choice is at the researcher's discretion, and it is a compromise between desirable properties in certain parameters and a broader qualitative inspection of the different solutions by the research domain's experts, which is a good practice when dealing quantitatively with data of a qualitative nature (i.e., texts) (Rizzoli et al, 2023;Sciandra et al., 2023). Both high level of held-out likelihood and low level of residuals can be considered a desirable parameter (Roberts et al., 2019;Sciandra et al., 2023).

The same goes for both high level of semantic coherence and word exclusivity (Roberts et al., 2014). Looking at Figure 2, we evaluated the models that meet these criteria span from six to eleven topics. We then inspected such solutions qualitatively (by visualizing the expected topic proportions and most associated words) and chose to proceed with nine topics. We estimated the STM with nine topics, incorporating the 'level' variable as a covariate in the topic prevalence component of the model. Furthermore, we applied spline normalization to the 'year' variable, taking into account the potential combined influence of the temporal dimension. This approach aims to capture any non-linear patterns or relationships between the 'year' variable and the prevalence of topics across documents. Although a 10-year period may not be extensive enough to detect substantial temporal variation, we made the deliberate choice to incorporate the potential influence of this variable in the model. We selected this specific time span due to its inclusion of several significant moments in the energy transition, warranting its consideration.

Each of the identified topics has been defined and labelled by researchers by exploring (i) articles and (ii) words highly likely to be associated with the topics, (iii) highest ranking FREX words (i.e., the harmonic mean of the word's rank in terms of exclusivity and frequency; Bischof & Airoldi, 2012), words characteristic of a topic as they appear less frequently in other topics both by (iv) lift score (i.e., words weighted by their overall frequency and how exclusive they are to the topic) and (v) a score given by the division of the log frequency of the word in the topic by the log frequency of the word in other topics (Roberts et al., 2019; see Appendix 1). Using a function that displays the mean topic proportions for the levels of the 'level' variable, we displayed topics variation according to national, regional, and local perspectives. Given that in estimating the STM we are assuming that factors (i.e., 'level' variable) influence the degree of association of a document to a topic, this representation illustrates the extent to which documents (i.e., articles) within a specific topic are associated with different geographical scales of the newspaper, basically estimating a regression where topic proportions are the outcome variable (Roberts et al., 2019). Moreover, to explore the difference between sources, namely taking into account the respective political orientation of the two regional newspapers, we applied the same calculation on the four

newspapers (thus by splitting the regional level of the 'level' variable, including both La Nuova Sardegna e L'Unione Sarda newspapers, into two).

The second analytical step aims at identifying the connotative component of representation and the way in which identity categories are related to the different topics identified. For text mining, we used ex-ante approaches (searching for keywords about identity according to the context analysis, e.g., worker, Sardinian, miner) as well as ex-post approaches (data-driven, i.e., selecting other words pertaining to identity from the ones highly associated with each topic). Starting from the 15 most likely associated article for each topic (for a total of 135 articles), we searched for the selected keywords through text mining, and extracted the texts in which they appear. This passage allowed us to identify which identity categories are invoked (i.e., referring to the semiotic triangle ego-alter-object, who is the 'ego' and who is the 'alter') against the various perspectives on energy issues that emerged from each topic (i.e., the social 'object'). sections.paper_id: Rizzoli et al. - 2024 - The identity‐attitude nexus in the representation of energy transition in a coal region (Sulcis, Ita
paragraphs: The topic 3 links the energy issue to the crisis of the metal-processing industries and the related decline in employment rates. Words such as Alcoa, Sider Alloys refer to the industries involved in a series of closures, takeovers, and transformations that characterized the crisis of the Portovesme industrial cluster (the concerned area in the Sulcis)allegedly caused by the global crisis and energy costs-with disputes lasting over 10 years. The topic is covered by the regional newspapers (mostly La Nuova Sardegna; Figure 5), but, above all, by national newspapers (Figure 4). The identities that are made salient are those of the workers (in Italian: lavoratori, operai) opposed to the national government, which is urged to intervene.

sections.header_raw: Topic 8: Loss of essentiality of thermoelectric power plants
sections.header_norm: topic 8 loss of essentiality of thermoelectric power plants
paragraphs: In Topic 8, the transition is viewed through the lens of the debate over the essentiality of thermal power plants. These plants are portrayed as pivotal for preserving jobs and the stability of the energy system, as well as for a non-traumatic transition to a new, unspecified energy model. The word essentiality is central and refers to the removal of the essentiality regime of the Ottana, Fiumesanto, and Portovesme power plants (three main poles of power generation in Sardinia) following the request made by Terna (transmission network operator). 4 There is no substantial difference in how the topic is addressed in the three levels considered (Figure 4).

Here, the salient self-identity is that of the workers (losing their job), but the Sardinian identity (mainly meant as its production system) and Sardinian citizens (facing high energy costs) are often mentioned as well, while the 'others'-those in conflict with this identity-are the Terna operator who 'stole the job' and the State, which remains passive in the face of this situation. Metaphors of theft, war, and killing are mentioned, as in the following extracts.

sections.header_norm: topic 1 place based innovation regeneration and economic development
paragraphs: Topic 1 depicts energy issues through the revitalization of the coal mines and the recovery of the brownfields. Once again this is aimed at avoiding an economic meltdown of the territory. In particular, reference is made to the Monte Sinni coal mine in Nuraxi Figus (district of a municipality in Sulcis), the last coal mine in Italy (in activity until 2019) and Carbosulcis, the last company in charge of managing the coal mine.
In the levels of analysis, this topic is mainly dealt with at regional and national level (Figure 5).

sections.header_norm: topic 7 clean coal research and innovation
paragraphs: Topic 7 refers to projects and strategies to keep the coal industry alive (e.g., project, coal, plan). Therefore, the themes covered include decades of state subsidies financing regional companies and R&D centres such as Carbosulcis (a coal mine company) or Sotacarbo (a regional enterprise for R&D on coal and low-carbon technologies). Similarly, another aspect is the development of 'clean coal' and technologies for carbon capture, storage, and use. In this regard, the agreement between Sotacarbo and the China Energy Research Society (CERS) is also mentioned. As for the newspapers where this topic is most debated, the topic clearly emerges in the articles from L'Unione Sarda (Figure 5). As for the levels, the national and regional ones are slightly more prevalent (Figure 4).

The salient identity when talking about the extractive industries is strictly local: the inhabitants of Sulcis, or 'sulcitani' in Italian, as opposed to the decision-makers. This relationship also implies a dependence of the Sulcitans on decision-makers, who should bring life back to Sulcis or, even better, find a cure for it. This metaphor of healing implies a vision of locals as patients who take on a passive role and need help.

sections.paper_id: Rizzoli et al. - 2024 - The identity‐attitude nexus in the representation of energy transition in a coal region (Sulcis, Ita
paragraphs: Topic 9 defines transition as the coal-to-gas transition, through methane pipeline or coastal deposits with regasification plants, as a solution for decarbonization. In fact, words as methane, coal, gas, methanization, pipeline, decarbonization characterize this topic, which is more locally debated and (slightly less) regionally debated (Figure 4). A closer look at the articles most likely to be associated with the topic shows that the coal-to-gas transition is not connotated in a unique way. Instead, the attitude towards this topic is polarized, and this vision of transition is both supported and contested.

The identity associated with the coal-to-gas representation is again that of the Sardinian citizen (we, Sardinians). On the one hand, methanization is seen as a good solution especially for the savings of Sardinian citizens, the autonomy of Sardinians, and the freedom from 'imprisonment' . On the other hand, the Sardinian citizens are depicted as a population who actively opposes this solution when it is presented as ill-fitting for a sustainable transition, as it would cause the destruction of their territory.

[. . . ] We will never be masters of our own future. Just as for transport matters, where we are equally cut off, it is essential that Sardinians also govern energy, or else we will always remain hostage to someone else.
La Nuova Sardegna, 2019
[. . . ] we want it [the methane in Sardinia] to the extent to which it really serves Sardinians and our businesses.
La Provincia del Sulcis Iglesiente, 2019
In the second case, the emphasis is on the dispossession of territory by 'others' that serve their own interests.
[. . . ] in Sardinia, on the other hand, too many seem only interested in turning our island into a gas distributor for third parties.
La Provincia del Sulcis Iglesiente, 2020

paragraphs: Topic 6: Smart grid (storage, efficiency, and distribution)

Topic 6 refers to the efficient management of the power system and the integration of renewable energy into the grid. In particular, it refers to smart grids (i.e., smart networks for remote control, regulation, management, monitoring, and optimization of energy consumption), which require a change in how the electricity grid is managed and used (e.g., distribution, accumulator, buildings, efficiency) and involves changes in users' practice as well as in the relevant public and private institutions and research centres (public buildings, Sotacarbo, universities). On this topic, smart cities (and communities) become the image of the energy future. This topic is most relevant in the local newspaper, as shown in Figure 4.

The salient identity here is that of 'Sardinian' (Sardinian citizen, the region of Sardinia), an identity broader than that of the Sulcis area. It is linked through debate (and depends on) the European community, which invested in Sardinia to finalize the smart grid, as exemplified by the paragraphs presented below.

Sardinia confronts Europe on the subject of energy efficiency in public administration buildings. It does so with projects and good practices involving the region, regional authorities and agencies, municipalities, trade associations, regional ESCO and schools.

paragraphs: Topic 5 deals with the outcomes of energy subsidies and investments, often with a negative connotation in terms of fraud and speculation.

It also includes specific projects, such as ARIA (a project carried out inside the mine for the distillation of argon, a chemical element useful for the search for dark matter) or eco-bonuses for energy efficiency in buildings. The energy issue is thus declined in terms of economic investments and outcomes (incentives, projects). In this topic, there is no significant difference in relation to the levels of the newspapers that cover it (nor in terms of which newspapers; cf. Figures 4 and 5).

As for identity, Sardinians are mentioned either as a group that benefits from these subsidies for building energy upgrades, or as fraudsters, who have exploited these bonuses in illegal ways. This topic is slightly more debated at the local level (Figure 4).

sections.paper_id: Rizzoli et al. - 2024 - The identity‐attitude nexus in the representation of energy transition in a coal region (Sulcis, Ita
paragraphs: Since 2012, the Sulcis area has been subject to plans and investments aimed at lifting it out of the industrial crisis and promoting a sustainable energy transition. The ongoing transition is not only touching upon economic and environmental issues, but it is also strictly intertwined with psychosocial aspects: it challenges an identity which was built during the last century around the mining history and industrial culture, and this challenge could hinder the effectiveness of transition plans (Bonaiuto et al., 1996;Brown & Perkins, 1992;Devine-Wright & Howes, 2010). Drawing upon SRT, we analysed national, regional, and local newspaper articles discussing the energy issue in Sulcis, thus placing our analysis at a societal level, and explored how the same object (i.e., the energy issue) was constructed differently (denotatively and connotatively) according to the salient identities and parties involved in the debate (Castro & Batel, 2008;Doise, 1989;Howarth, 2006).

We identified nine topics corresponding to different aspects of the energy issue. The most discussed topic (i.e., topic 3) is focussed on the industrial crisis and on job losses. Despite talking about sustainable transition, the major point of discussion is not the need for change the energy system, but the crisis this entails (e.g., topic 8). This result suggests a cultural manifestation of the economy-environment themata (Marková, 2000) where economic considerations prevail on and completely obscure climate and environmental ones (Brondi et al., 2014).

When the energy issue comes to the forefront, we observed how different categorizations of self and others are evoked, with their respective connotations (Mason et al., 2014). When the content of the representation concerns the reconversion and redevelopment of the coal mines, the identity mentioned is strictly limited to miners and does not include other workers and professionals (such as engineers, technicians, etc.) (e.g., topic 1). When coal is presented as an indispensable source for energy supply also within a sustainable transition (e.g., topic 7), the local identity is evoked: coal is connected with 'Sulcitani' , the inhabitants of Sulcis. Constructing the transition of coal mines or the use of coal as something exclusively related to miners and to the region's inhabitants, respectively, replicates the overlap between workers and miners, coal and Sulcis, provides continuity to social identity and strengthens the place-identity link. By erasing other professions, skills, and opportunities linked with reconversion and transition projects, this representation of the people and the territory offers no alternative other than the extractivist status quo (e.g., Della Bosca & Gillespie, 2018). Instead, when the level of categorization shifts from being 'Sulcitan' to 'Sardinian' new themes emerge, such as smart grid, investments, and subsidies for energy efficiency and renewable energies. Overall, the different topics suggest that the main theme is how to make the region economically competitive in terms of energy production (e.g., topic 9, topic 8, topic 1) rather than how to reach a socially and environmentally sustainable transition. When the Sardinian superordinate identity becomes salient, the energy transition assumes an ambivalent or negative connotation for several reasons, such as land defacement (topic 4), lack of infrastructure (topics 6 and 9), insufficient political action (topic 2), or frauds in the use of incentives (topic 5).

Finally, when the broader 'citizens' identity is salient in the articles, the citizens themselves are portrayed as passive, dependent, or as victims. Citizens are represented as dispossessed (e.g., topic 9), 'attacked' by companies or authorities (e.g., topic 8), or as those who are not 'taken care of' by politics ('the others'; e.g., topic 7, topic 3).

This happens irrespective of the level of the press, local, regional, or national. However, it should be noted that the topics where the local identity is the most salient are the topics most discussed in regional and national newspapers (topics 1 and 7). Nevertheless, the salience of the Sardinian superordinate identity in association with sustainable energy is also brought up by the local newspaper analysed (e.g., topic 6). The way in which these dynamics between the parties are reported in the articles thus promotes an idea of distance between decision-makers and the community, with the latter being ignored or at best presented as a passive recipient of decisions taken elsewhere, in line with the deficit model (Batel & Devine-Wright, 2015).

sections.paper_id: Rizzoli et al. - 2024 - The identity‐attitude nexus in the representation of energy transition in a coal region (Sulcis, Ita
paragraphs: The promise of Social Representation Theory is to go beyond the 'impact' and 'acceptance' approaches to energy transition. SRT allows us to investigate how attitudes towards energy issues are intertwined with media communication in the public arena, and how they are related to power and identity relationships (Batel & Devine-Wright, 2015;Devine-Wright & Devine-Wright, 2009;Sarrica et al., 2015;Upham et al., 2015). Starting from this premise, in the present study we used an innovative methodological approach and analysed which alternative visions of the energy transition currently circulate on newspapers, and how these visions are constructed in relationship with different nested identities. Furthermore, we situated our study in a prototypical coal and carbon intensive region (Sulcis, Italy), where phase-out policies also involve a destabilization-reconfiguration of communities. Overall, the results confirm what has been observed from other perspectives and in other contexts (Cislak et al., 2018). In the newspapers we analysed, alternative representations of the energy transition are conveyed with positive or negative connotations according to the way in which identities are made salient at the expense of other actors. These dynamics change the representational objects and the ego-alter relationship (Mason et al., 2014). We found a number of mechanisms of exclusion of perspectives and voices from the local communities, similarly to what is reported in literature related to Italy as well (Batel & Devine-Wright, 2015;Brondi et al., 2014). We did not investigate directly whether this is a case of cultural manipulation (Bell & Yorke, 2010); nevertheless, the overlap between workers and miners, the mention of Sulcitanian identity when talking about coal, the categorization in superordinate terms when talking about innovation, are all functional elements to power relations inherent to the place in question. In this sense, what is presented as a sustainable transition seems to replicate processes of domination and injustice. Further in-depth investigations, informed by critical and post-colonial approaches, are needed to shed light on such mechanisms (Batel & Devine-Wright, 2016;Lennon, 2017;Sovacool et al., 2019). This seems all the more necessary when we consider that, according to our study, not even local newspapers seem to be able to offer radically alternative representations to those found at regional and national level, suggesting possible processes of disempowerment and stigmatization (Elcheroth et al., 2011;Howarth, 2001Howarth, , 2006)).

From a methodological point of view, the use of NLP methods allowed the operationalization of some of the assumptions derived from SRT, namely with regard to the societal level of analysis, exploration of public discourse, presence of alternative self-other-object semiotic triangles (Bauer & Gaskell, 2008;Howarth et al., 2013), and support of the interpretative activity with rigorous and systematic analyses. The first step of the analyses provided a quick and effective summary of the contents (denotative and connotative elements) that are conveyed in the social arena. The use of such semi-automatic approaches allowed us to address a large amount of textual data and simultaneously take into account associated variables such as the level of the press (i.e., local, regional, national). It should be noted that methods relying on NLP are largely used in SRT. However, STM provides a level of granularity which advances the studies in this field. This approach presents some well-known limitations, which are inherent to the difficulty, for example, to grasp the pragmatic dimension of communication. For this reason, these methods are often used in conjunction with some more qualitative analysis. In this study, we also associated STM with a qualitative focus on some of the results. Indeed, the simple mention of self-other categorization could not have been interpreted without in-depth understanding of how various identities are used, mobilized, and evoked to reproduce or challenge situated intergroup and power relationships. Further analyses could deepen other representational and communication features that we were forced to overlooked in the present study. Nevertheless, the application of STM was essential to carry out the qualitative analysis in a focussed and rigorous manner. The systematicity and reproducibility of results could benefit other scholars interested in examining the relationships between the social construction of reality, attitudes, and identity, which is particularly relevant for socially debated and politically sensitive issues such as energy transition.

In the newspapers we analysed, alternative representations of the energy transition are conveyed with positive or negative connotations according to the way in which identities are made salient at the expense of other actors. These dynamics change the representational objects and the ego-alter relationship (Mason et al., 2014). We found a number of mechanisms of exclusion of perspectives and voices from the local communities, similarly to what is reported in literature related to Italy as well (Batel & Devine-Wright, 2015;Brondi et al., 2014). We did not investigate directly whether this is a case of cultural manipulation (Bell & Yorke, 2010); nevertheless, the overlap between workers and miners, the mention of Sulcitanian identity when talking about coal, the categorization in superordinate terms when talking about innovation, are all functional elements to power relations inherent to the place in question. In this sense, what is presented as a sustainable transition seems to replicate processes of domination and injustice. Further in-depth investigations, informed by critical and post-colonial approaches, are needed to shed light on such mechanisms (Batel & Devine-Wright, 2016;Lennon, 2017;Sovacool et al., 2019). This seems all the more necessary when we consider that, according to our study, not even local newspapers seem to be able to offer radically alternative representations to those found at regional and national level, suggesting possible processes of disempowerment and stigmatization (Elcheroth et al., 2011;Howarth, 2001Howarth, , 2006)).

From a methodological point of view, the use of NLP methods allowed the operationalization of some of the assumptions derived from SRT, namely with regard to the societal level of analysis, exploration of public discourse, presence of alternative self-other-object semiotic triangles (Bauer & Gaskell, 2008;Howarth et al., 2013), and support of the interpretative activity with rigorous and systematic analyses. The first step of the analyses provided a quick and effective summary of the contents (denotative and connotative elements) that are conveyed in the social arena. The use of such semi-automatic approaches allowed us to address a large amount of textual data and simultaneously take into account associated variables such as the level of the press (i.e., local, regional, national). It should be noted that methods relying on NLP are largely used in SRT. However, STM provides a level of granularity which advances the studies in this field. This approach presents some well-known limitations, which are inherent to the difficulty, for example, to grasp the pragmatic dimension of communication. For this reason, these methods are often used in conjunction with some more qualitative analysis. In this study, we also associated STM with a qualitative focus on some of the results. Indeed, the simple mention of self-other categorization could not have been interpreted without in-depth understanding of how various identities are used, mobilized, and evoked to reproduce or challenge situated intergroup and power relationships. Further analyses could deepen other representational and communication features that we were forced to overlooked in the present study. Nevertheless, the application of STM was essential to carry out the qualitative analysis in a focussed and rigorous manner. The systematicity and reproducibility of results could benefit other scholars interested in examining the relationships between the social construction of reality, attitudes, and identity, which is particularly relevant for socially debated and politically sensitive issues such as energy transition.
